questions.specification: A telecommunications company implements network management and automation tools to modernize their network infrastructure.

parts.content: Compare traditional networking architecture with a software-defined, centrally managed network architecture, discussing the differences in control/management, packet forwarding, configuration methods, flexibility, and vendor dependencies.
parts.markscheme:
- Contrast: Traditional networking uses a distributed approach to decision-making/management across individual devices whereas a centrally managed approach uses a central management system/controller for network-wide management <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Contrast: Traditional devices combine decision-making/management functions with packet forwarding whereas a centrally managed approach separates management/decision-making from packet forwarding on devices <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Contrast: Traditional configuration relies on manual per-device configuration (e.g. CLI) whereas a centrally managed approach enables automated, programmatic configuration via APIs/templates <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Contrast: Traditional networks have limited flexibility for rapid change whereas centrally managed networks offer higher programmability/automation for faster service deployment and scaling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Contrast: Traditional networking can lead to vendor lock-in whereas centrally managed/software-defined approaches can reduce dependency through use of open standards/interoperability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain: Traditional networks can result in complex, fragmented management whereas central management can streamline operations and enable faster, consistent changes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyse the security implications of centralizing network control/management in a controller/management system, considering both benefits and risks.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify: Centralized control/management can create a significant single point of failure risk for the entire network <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify: Centralized visibility provides a holistic view that enables more effective security monitoring and anomaly detection <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain: If the central controller/management system is compromised, an attacker could potentially affect large parts of the network (e.g. change configurations/policies) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain: Centralized management allows rapid and consistent deployment of security policies/configuration updates across the whole network in response to threats <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A multinational bank implements a hybrid cloud architecture connecting on-premises data centres with public cloud services.

parts.content: Explain the networking considerations for deploying core banking systems on-premises while hosting web applications and data analytics in public cloud environments. Discuss connectivity methods, security levels, and latency requirements.
parts.markscheme:
- Core banking requires ultra-low latency private line connections for real-time transactions. <marks>2 mark</marks>
- Web applications can use internet connectivity with appropriate security measures. <marks>2 mark</marks>
- Data analytics requires dedicated connections to handle large data transfers securely. <marks>2 mark</marks>
- API gateways are needed at the edge/DMZ for service orchestration with high security. <marks>2 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyse the challenges of maintaining data consistency and security across hybrid cloud environments.
parts.markscheme:
- Network latency affects real-time data synchronization between environments. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encryption overhead impacts performance of inter-cloud communications. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Network partitioning can cause data consistency issues. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compliance requirements may restrict data movement across network boundaries. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A video streaming service implements a distributed architecture to handle millions of concurrent users.

parts.content:
(a) Complete the load balancing strategy comparison table:

| Load Balancing Method | Decision Criteria | Response Time | Scalability | Complexity | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Round Robin | — | Low | — | Low | — |
| Least Connections | Active connections | — | Medium | Medium | — |
| Weighted Round Robin | Different server capacities | — | — | Medium | Server variability |
| Geographic | — | Low | High | High | — |
| Content-based | — | Medium | — | High | — | [5]
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the Round Robin criteria as sequential, scalability as medium, and use case for equal server capacity <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the Least Connections response time as variable and use case for long-lived connections <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the Weighted Round Robin response time as predictable and scalability as high <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the Geographic criteria as user location and use case for global applications <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the Content-based criteria as URL/header analysis and scalability as high for microservices <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Define Round Robin Strategy
steps.content:
In a distributed architecture, **Round Robin** is the simplest load-balancing algorithm. It passes each new request to the next server in a fixed, sequential list. 

Because it does not check the current load or capacity of the servers, it works best when all servers have **equal capacity**. While simple to implement (Low Complexity), its **Scalability** is considered **Medium** because it can become inefficient if tasks vary greatly in processing requirements.

highlights.text: Identify the Round Robin criteria as sequential, scalability as medium, and use case for equal server capacity

steps.title: Analyze Least Connections
steps.content:
The **Least Connections** method is more dynamic. It tracks how many **active connections** each server is currently handling and sends the new request to the server with the lowest count.

Since connections might last for different durations (e.g., one user watching a 2-minute clip vs. another a 2-hour movie), the **Response Time** is **Variable**. This method is the **Best Use Case** for **long-lived connections**, which is common in video streaming services.

highlights.text: Identify the Least Connections response time as variable and use case for long-lived connections

steps.title: Examine Weighted Round Robin
steps.content:
For a streaming service using a mix of older and newer hardware, **Weighted Round Robin** is used. Each server is assigned a weight based on its processing power.

By directing more traffic to more powerful servers, the **Response Time** becomes more **Predictable**. This offers **High Scalability** because the system can easily integrate new, high-performance servers into the existing cluster without overwhelming older ones.

highlights.text: Identify the Weighted Round Robin response time as predictable and scalability as high

steps.title: Evaluate Geographic Load Balancing
steps.content:
To minimize latency for a global audience, **Geographic** load balancing is used. The **Decision Criteria** is based on the **user's physical location** (usually determined via IP address).

By routing a user in London to a server in Europe rather than the US, the service achieves Low Response Time. This is the essential strategy for **global applications** to ensure a smooth streaming experience regardless of the user's region.

highlights.text: Identify the Geographic criteria as user location and use case for global applications

steps.title: Assess Content-based Balancing
steps.content:
In complex distributed systems, **Content-based** (or Layer 7) load balancing looks at the actual request data. The **Decision Criteria** involves **URL or header analysis**.

For example, requests for high-definition video files might be sent to one specialized server cluster, while account login requests go to another. This provides **High Scalability for microservices**, where different parts of the application are handled by different dedicated server groups.

highlights.text: Identify the Content-based criteria as URL/header analysis and scalability as high for microservices

steps.title: Finalized Comparison Table
steps.content:
Based on the theoretical analysis above, here is the completed table for the 5 marks:

| Load Balancing Method | Decision Criteria | Response Time | Scalability | Complexity | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Round Robin | **Sequential** | Low | **Medium** | Low | **Equal capacity** |
| Least Connections | Active connections | **Variable** | Medium | Medium | **Long-lived connections** |
| Weighted Round Robin | Server capacities | **Predictable** | **High** | Medium | Server variability |
| Geographic | **User location** | Low | High | High | **Global apps** |
| Content-based | **URL/Header analysis**| Medium | **High** | High | **Microservices** |

steps.title: Analyze Round Robin (Low State)
steps.content:
In **Round Robin**, the load balancer works through a list of servers sequentially. This method has the lowest **computational overhead** because the balancer does not need to monitor the health or current load of the servers; it simply sends the next request to the next server in line. 

Because of this simplicity, it is best used when all servers have **equal capacity**. While response time is low due to minimal processing by the balancer, its scalability is considered **Medium** because it may inadvertently send a heavy request to a server already struggling with a complex task.

highlights.text: Identify the Round Robin criteria as sequential, scalability as medium, and use case for equal server capacity

steps.title: Least Connections Performance Trade-off
steps.content:
The **Least Connections** method requires the load balancer to maintain a real-time table of **active connections**. This increase in **resource management** (tracking state) means the load balancer does more work than in Round Robin. 

Consequently, the **Response Time** is **Variable**; the balancer must perform a lookup before routing. However, this is the ideal strategy for **long-lived connections** (like a 2-hour video stream) because it ensures no single server becomes a bottleneck, even if some streams last much longer than others.

highlights.text: Identify the Least Connections response time as variable and use case for long-lived connections

steps.title: Weighted Round Robin Scalability
steps.content:
In a distributed video service, servers often have **different capacities** (e.g., some have more RAM or faster CPUs). **Weighted Round Robin** assigns a "weight" to each server. 

From a performance perspective, this offers **Predictable** response times because traffic is distributed proportionally to what the hardware can handle. This leads to **High Scalability**, as the service can easily add new, more powerful servers to the cluster and simply assign them a higher weight without reconfiguring the entire logic.

highlights.text: Identify the Weighted Round Robin response time as predictable and scalability as high

steps.title: Geographic Latency Management
steps.content:
The **Geographic** strategy uses the **user's location** (IP address) as the decision criterion. While the initial lookup of the user's region adds some **complexity** (High), the trade-off is a significantly lower latency for the end-user.

By routing a user in Tokyo to a server in Tokyo rather than New York, physical distance—and therefore response time—is minimized. This is the standard for **global applications** that must maintain a high Quality of Service (QoS) across different continents.

highlights.text: Identify the Geographic criteria as user location and use case for global applications

steps.title: Content-based Inspection Overhead
steps.content:
**Content-based** balancing involves **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** to analyze **URL or HTTP headers**. This has the highest **computational overhead** because the load balancer must open the packet and read its contents before making a routing decision (leading to Medium response times).

Despite this overhead, it allows for **High Scalability** in **microservices** architectures. For example, requests for `/video` can be sent to optimized storage servers, while `/login` requests go to high-security authentication servers, allowing each service to scale independently.

highlights.text: Identify the Content-based criteria as URL/header analysis and scalability as high for microservices

steps.title: Final Summary Table
steps.content:
Based on the performance trade-off analysis, here is the completed table:

| Load Balancing Method | Decision Criteria | Response Time | Scalability | Complexity | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Round Robin | **Sequential** | Low | **Medium** | Low | **Equal server capacity** |
| Least Connections | Active connections | **Variable** | Medium | Medium | **Long-lived connections** |
| Weighted Round Robin | Different server capacities | **Predictable** | **High** | Medium | Server variability |
| Geographic | **User location** | Low | High | High | **Global applications** |
| Content-based | **URL/header analysis** | Medium | **High** | High | **Microservices** |

steps.title: Analyze simple sequential distribution
steps.content:
Using the **Scenario-to-Criteria Mapping** approach, we first look at a scenario where all servers in the cluster have the same hardware specifications. In this environment, the simplest method is to send requests one after another.

For **Round Robin**, the decision is made **sequentially**. Because it does not account for the current load on a server, its scalability is considered **medium**. It is most effective when there is **equal server capacity** across the network.

highlights.text: Identify the Round Robin criteria as sequential, scalability as medium, and use case for equal server capacity

steps.title: Mapping long-lived connections
steps.content:
In a video streaming scenario, some users might watch a 2-hour movie while others watch a 30-second clip. This creates "long-lived connections."

**Least Connections** tracks **active connections** to ensure no single server is overwhelmed by too many persistent streams. Because the duration of these streams varies, the **response time** is **variable**. This method is the best choice for environments with **long-lived connections**.

highlights.text: Identify the Least Connections response time as variable and use case for long-lived connections

steps.title: Handling server hardware variability
steps.content:
Modern data centers often have a mix of older and newer servers. To maximize efficiency, we map the load to the server's specific capacity.

**Weighted Round Robin** uses weights to send more traffic to powerful servers. This results in **predictable response times** because the work is distributed according to what the hardware can handle. This approach offers **high scalability**, allowing the service to easily integrate new, more powerful nodes into the existing cluster.

highlights.text: Identify the Weighted Round Robin response time as predictable and scalability as high

steps.title: Mapping global user distribution
steps.content:
For a global streaming service, physical distance (latency) is a major factor. If a user is in Tokyo, sending their request to a server in London would be inefficient.

**Geographic Load Balancing** uses the **user location** (often via IP address) as the decision criteria. This is the essential strategy for **global applications** to ensure the lowest possible latency and high availability.

highlights.text: Identify the Geographic criteria as user location and use case for global applications

steps.title: Supporting microservices architecture
steps.content:
Video streaming platforms often use microservices (e.g., one service for search, another for user profiles, and another for the actual video data). 

**Content-based** load balancing analyzes the **URL or header** of the request to route it to the specific service required. This mapping is highly effective for **microservices**, allowing each service to scale independently based on demand.

highlights.text: Identify the Content-based criteria as URL/header analysis and scalability as high for microservices

steps.title: Final Table Summary
steps.content:
Based on our mapping, the completed table looks like this:

| Load Balancing Method | Decision Criteria | Response Time | Scalability | Complexity | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Round Robin** | Sequential | Low | Medium | Low | Equal server capacity |
| **Least Connections** | Active connections | Variable | Medium | Medium | Long-lived connections |
| **Weighted Round Robin** | Different server capacities | Predictable | High | Medium | Server variability |
| **Geographic** | User location | Low | High | High | Global applications |
| **Content-based** | URL/header analysis | Medium | High | High | Microservices |

parts.content: (b) Explain how Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) work with load balancers to optimize video streaming performance globally. [2]
parts.markscheme:
- Explain how CDNs cache content at edge locations closer to users to reduce latency and origin server load <marks>A1</marks>

- Explain how load balancers distribute incoming traffic among these edge servers to ensure optimal performance and prevent congestion <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the two distinct challenges
steps.content: To handle millions of users globally, a streaming service must solve two main problems: **physical distance** (which causes delay) and **server overload** (which causes crashes). This is where Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) and load balancers play their specific roles.

steps.title: CDN: Reducing physical distance
steps.content:
The **CDN's role** is to bring the content geographically closer to the user. It does this by caching (storing copies) of the video on "edge servers" located in various cities around the world.

By serving the video from a nearby edge server rather than a distant central origin server, the **latency** (the delay before the video starts) is significantly reduced. This also protects the origin server from having to handle every single request.

highlights.text: Explain how CDNs cache content at edge locations closer to users to reduce latency and origin server load

steps.title: Load Balancer: Managing traffic
steps.content:
Even with edge servers, a popular video might attract thousands of local users at once. The **load balancer's role** is to act as a traffic cop. 

It receives incoming requests and distributes them across multiple servers at that location. This ensures that no single server is overwhelmed, preventing **congestion** and maintaining a smooth streaming experience even during peak traffic times.

highlights.text: Explain how load balancers distribute incoming traffic among these edge servers to ensure optimal performance and prevent congestion

steps.title: Summary of functional roles
steps.content:
In summary, they optimize performance through complementary roles:
- **CDNs** solve the geographic problem by reducing the distance data travels.
- **Load balancers** solve the capacity problem by managing how many requests each server handles.

This combination ensures that a user in Tokyo and a user in New York both receive high-quality video without buffering.

questions.specification: A global content delivery network (CDN) optimizes web content distribution across multiple geographic regions.

parts.content:
Complete the following CDN architecture analysis table:

| CDN Component | Location | Primary Function | Caching Strategy | Performance Benefit | Failure Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Origin Server | — | Master content storage | — | — | High |
| Edge Cache | ISP locations | — | LRU, TTL-based | — | Low |
| Regional Cache | — | Intermediate storage | — | Reduced origin load | — |
| DNS Resolver | Global | — | Geographic routing | — | Medium |
parts.markscheme:
- **Origin Server**: Location: Data centres; Caching Strategy: No caching; Performance Benefit: Authoritative source <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Edge Cache**: Primary Function: Local content delivery; Performance Benefit: Reduced latency <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Regional Cache**: Location: Major cities; Caching Strategy: Popular content; Failure Impact: Medium <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **DNS Resolver**: Primary Function: Traffic direction; Performance Benefit: Optimal server selection <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Explain how anycast routing improves CDN performance and state one potential limitation.
parts.markscheme:
- Anycast uses the same IP address advertised from multiple locations so user requests are routed to the nearest/lowest-cost available server automatically <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This improves performance by reducing latency (shorter physical distance/fewer network hops) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Anycast can improve availability by automatically rerouting traffic if one site/server becomes unavailable <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation stated, e.g. possible session disruption/instability if routing changes during a stateful connection <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A cloud service provider designs a scalable network architecture to support microservices applications.

parts.content: Analyse how each layer of the OSI model is utilized in microservices communication, focusing on the Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, and Network layers. Include specific protocols and scaling considerations for each layer.
parts.markscheme:
- **Application Layer**: utilization of protocols like HTTP for service requests and APIs <marks>1 mark</marks> and scaling via load balancing and/or API gateways <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Presentation Layer**: utilization of SSL/TLS for data encryption (and data format translation if relevant) <marks>1 mark</marks> and scaling through reducing payload size (e.g. compression) to improve throughput <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Session Layer**: utilization of establishing, maintaining and ending communication sessions (e.g. managing client-server dialogues) <marks>1 mark</marks> and scaling via stateless design and/or avoiding server-side session storage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Transport Layer**: utilization of TCP (reliable delivery) or UDP (lower overhead) <marks>1 mark</marks> and scaling through connection management (e.g. keep-alive/connection pooling) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Network Layer**: utilization of IP for routing and addressing <marks>1 mark</marks> and scaling through efficient subnetting/routing design to reduce congestion and improve addressing efficiency <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how containerization platforms change traditional network architecture requirements compared to monolithic applications.
parts.markscheme:
- More frequent changes to endpoints means more reliance on dynamic addressing/service location rather than fixed addresses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Greater need for isolation/segmentation between services (e.g. separate networks or firewall rules) due to many small components <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased internal (service-to-service) traffic compared to a monolithic design, affecting capacity planning and monitoring <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased importance of automated configuration/management because manually configuring many small services is impractical <marks>1 mark</marks>
